questions.scenario: The exports team wants to delete temporary retry files under `tmp/` from Blob Storage. The lifecycle policy says temporary files can be deleted after seven days, and the current files are from the last hour. What should happen?

options.text: Do not delete those current files under the seven-day rule; confirm whether they are still part of the retry workflow.
Delete them immediately because every path named tmp is safe to remove.
Move them into SQL so backups protect them longer.
Disable lifecycle management so no file is ever removed automatically.

questions.explanation: Retention rules need to match the actual age and workflow. A tmp prefix is not enough reason to delete current retry data.

questions.scenario: A support engineer asks to restore a single order record after a bad manual update. The database has long-term retention, but the app also wrote receipt files and idempotency keys during the same period. What is the best response?

options.text: Treat the restore as an application recovery task and check related data stores before changing production data.
Restore only SQL because the order record is the main business fact.
Delete matching blobs and keys so SQL becomes the only source of truth.
Use the file share snapshot because file shares can recover all Azure data services.

questions.explanation: Recovery often crosses service boundaries. SQL may be the main record, but related blobs and keys may affect what users and support see.
